profile.title: The Initiated Reader
profile.summary: Your instincts are already moving beyond surface symbolism. You understand that position shapes meaning, that cards in combination create narrative, and that the useful question is rarely what a card "means" in isolation.
profile.startPoint: Begin directly with spread practice. The Minor Arcana will be your focus — you don't need the Major Arcana foundations.

currentQuizCards.name: The Death
currentQuizCards.upright: Death rarely means death. In nearly every context it signals an ending that makes a beginning possible — the kind of change that is so complete there is no going back. Something has run its course. The grief is real, but so is what comes after. In a reading, Death asks what you are clinging to that has already ended, and what would become possible if you let it go.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Resistance to necessary change, clinging to what is already over. Or stagnation so complete that even ending has stalled.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Fool: profound transformation leading to a new beginning. Next to The Tower: sudden, devastating change. Next to The Ten of Cups: the end of a chapter but not of love.

scenario.love: A relationship that has fundamentally changed, or needs to end for both people to grow.
scenario.work: The end of a role, a business phase, or an identity tied to work.
scenario.self: What in your sense of self needs to be allowed to die so something real can replace it?

currentQuizCards.name: The Hanged Man
currentQuizCards.upright: He hangs by one foot, at peace. He chose this. The Hanged Man is voluntarily suspended — not trapped but paused, deliberately, to see the world from a different angle. In readings he calls for surrender rather than struggle, for the kind of waiting that is actually doing something profound. What you cannot see from your current position becomes visible when you let go and invert.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Needless martyrdom, or suspension becoming stagnation. Refusing to make a necessary sacrifice. Waiting that has become avoidance.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Wheel: a pause before the next turn. Next to The Star: stillness that will lead to healing. Next to The Eight of Swords: paralysis dressed up as patience.

scenario.love: A relationship put on hold — or the moment of surrendering the need to control the outcome.
scenario.work: A project requires waiting. Push harder and you lose. Let it breathe.
scenario.self: What are you refusing to release your grip on?

currentQuizCards.name: The Empress
currentQuizCards.upright: The Empress is surrounded by nature in full abundance — wheat, water, a forest. She is pregnant with possibility. This is the card of creative fertility, of things coming to fruition, of the body, the earth, and the sensory world. She asks: where in your life is something ready to grow? She also speaks to the need for nurturing — of projects, relationships, or yourself.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Creative block, lack of nurturing, overdependence on others or on being needed. A mother who smothers. Fertility withheld. Reversed, The Empress asks where growth is being prevented, and why.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Emperor: complementary energies, or power imbalance in a relationship. Next to The Star: healing through reconnecting with the body and earth. Next to The Hanged Man: abundance put on hold.

scenario.love: Deep sensual connection, or the desire for it. Possibly pregnancy or the urge to create a home.
scenario.work: A creative project reaching maturity. Nurture it rather than forcing it.
scenario.self: Take care of the body. Rest, pleasure, and time in nature are not luxuries right now.

currentQuizCards.name: The Fool
currentQuizCards.upright: A new chapter is opening. The Fool carries no baggage — literally. He steps off the cliff not from ignorance but from trust. In a reading, this card asks whether you are willing to begin without knowing how it ends. It speaks to pure potential, unformed energy, the courage of not yet knowing.
currentQuizCards.reversed: The leap is being blocked — by fear, by overthinking, or by a reckless jump taken too soon. Reversed, The Fool asks: what are you refusing to begin, or what have you begun without any thought at all?
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The World: a full cycle ending and beginning. Next to The Tower: the leap may cost more than expected. Next to The High Priestess: trust your instincts before you act.

scenario.love: Beginning something new — a relationship, a feeling, a risk taken in vulnerability.
scenario.work: A career pivot, a new venture, or a moment when jumping in is the only way forward.
scenario.self: The invitation to stop rehearsing and start living.

currentQuizCards.name: The Chariot
currentQuizCards.upright: A woman closes the jaws of a lion — not with force but with gentleness. The infinity symbol floats above her. Strength is not about domination; it is about the courage to face what frightens us and transform it through love rather than will. This card speaks to the power of patience, of sustained inner work, of meeting the wild parts of yourself without flinching.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Self-doubt, lack of confidence, or strength being expressed as brutality rather than grace. An inner battle being lost to fear.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Chariot: the difference between force and real power. Next to The Devil: confronting an addiction or compulsion with compassion. Next to The Moon: facing the unconscious.

scenario.love: A relationship requiring patience and inner reserves rather than action. Or the courage to show vulnerability.
scenario.work: Sustained effort over time, not a dramatic gesture. You may not see results immediately.
scenario.self: What in you needs to be faced rather than suppressed?

currentQuizCards.name: The Hermit
currentQuizCards.upright: The Hermit stands on a mountain peak, lantern raised, illuminating only the next few steps. He has withdrawn from the world not in defeat but in search of something that only solitude can deliver. In a reading he calls for a period of genuine reflection — stepping back from noise, from others' opinions, to find your own counsel. The light is yours; it can only illuminate your own path.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Isolation becoming avoidance. Withdrawal that has curdled into disconnection. Or the refusal to spend any time alone — running from the self.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Star: solitude leading toward hope and recovery. Next to The World: the inner journey nearing completion. Next to The Six of Swords: moving away is the right choice right now.

scenario.love: A period of needing space — not rejection, but necessary distance for clarity.
scenario.work: A time for deep focus and single-minded work, away from committees and noise.
scenario.self: What does your own inner voice say when you stop listening to everyone else?

spread.name: Situation & Path
spread.description: Where you are, what opposes you, what helps.

card.name: The Lovers
card.upright: Despite the name, this card is fundamentally about choice — a significant one, where values must be clarified before a path is taken. The angel above the couple asks them to look at what they truly believe. In relationship readings it speaks to genuine union and deep alignment. But wherever it falls, the question is: does this choice reflect who you actually are?
card.reversed: Misalignment, a choice made from the wrong place, or avoidance of a necessary decision. Relationships out of harmony with personal values.
card.relational: Next to The Devil: desire versus values, addiction to a relationship dynamic. Next to The Tower: a choice that will break something. Next to The Two of Cups: deep mutual connection.

scenario.love: A significant relationship decision. Not whether to stay or go — that may be the surface. What do you actually value?
scenario.work: A choice between two paths that cannot both be taken. Check alignment before deciding.
scenario.self: What does love — of any kind — demand from you right now?

card.name: The Fool
card.upright: A new chapter is opening. The Fool carries no baggage — literally. He steps off the cliff not from ignorance but from trust. In a reading, this card asks whether you are willing to begin without knowing how it ends. It speaks to pure potential, unformed energy, the courage of not yet knowing.
card.reversed: The leap is being blocked — by fear, by overthinking, or by a reckless jump taken too soon. Reversed, The Fool asks: what are you refusing to begin, or what have you begun without any thought at all?
card.relational: Next to The World: a full cycle ending and beginning. Next to The Tower: the leap may cost more than expected. Next to The High Priestess: trust your instincts before you act.

scenario.love: Beginning something new — a relationship, a feeling, a risk taken in vulnerability.
scenario.work: A career pivot, a new venture, or a moment when jumping in is the only way forward.
scenario.self: The invitation to stop rehearsing and start living.

card.name: The Chariot
card.upright: Justice holds a sword and scales — clarity and balance. This is not poetic justice but the logical consequence of actions taken. In a reading it often points to legal matters, contracts, or situations where fairness and accountability are at stake. More broadly, it asks you to look honestly at your own conduct: what cause have you set in motion, and what effect are you prepared to meet?
card.reversed: Dishonesty, unfair treatment, avoiding accountability. Or a situation where justice is genuinely absent — and you know it.
card.relational: Next to The Emperor: institutional justice, law. Next to The Moon: deception clouding a fair outcome. Next to The Eight of Swords: self-imposed restriction from a fear of judgement.

scenario.love: A relationship requiring honesty about what is and isn't fair to each person.
scenario.work: Contracts, negotiations, performance reviews, or the consequences of past decisions arriving.
scenario.self: Where are you not being fully honest with yourself or others?

currentReading.querrant: A client has recently been made redundant after 12 years at the same company. They feel lost.

steps.prompt: You look at the spread. The Hierophant sits in the Past position. Before addressing the redundancy directly, what does this card tell you about how they experienced that 12-year period?

options.text: They valued the structure and identity that the institution gave them — perhaps too much.
options.chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

options.text: They were probably too conventional in how they worked — never challenged the rules.
options.chosen_response: That's a possible reading, but it's evaluative rather than curious. The Hierophant in past position more usefully asks: what did that structure mean to them? Judgement about whether they should have challenged it comes later, if at all.

options.text: Religious or cultural values shaped their career choices.
options.chosen_response: Possible, but you're narrowing too fast. The Hierophant's institutional dimension is broader than religion. The more useful question at this stage is: how much of their identity was tied to belonging to that particular institution?

steps._chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

steps.prompt: Death sits in the Present. How do you introduce this card to someone who is already frightened?

options.text: Immediately reassure them that Death never means actual death, then explain the transformation meaning.
options.chosen_response: The reassurance impulse is understandable, but leading with it actually increases anxiety — it signals there's something to be scared of. A better approach: name what is true. Something has ended. That is real, and it matters. Then open toward what becomes possible when something ends.

options.text: Acknowledge that something genuinely has ended, and that grief is the right response — then ask what they are still trying to hold onto.
options.chosen_response: Exactly. Death in the present isn't a threat — it's a description. Something has ended. The grief is real and worth honouring. But the productive question for a reading is: what are they still trying to sustain that has already gone? That's where the actual work lives.

options.text: Focus on transformation — this is a new beginning in disguise.
options.chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps._chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps.prompt: The Star closes the spread in the Future position. After what you've said about the Hierophant and Death, how do you read this?

options.text: Everything will be fine — the Star is a positive card and the future looks good.
options.chosen_response: Too easy. The Star is genuinely hopeful but it isn't a promise of outcome — it's a quality of possibility. Given that the Past shows deep institutional identity and the Present shows real loss, the Star in the future position is asking: can you let yourself hope for something that is genuinely yours, rather than something given to you by an institution?

options.text: Recovery and renewal are available — but the sequence suggests it requires genuinely releasing what's gone, not just waiting it out.
options.chosen_response: Good. The narrative arc across these three cards is: deep institutional belonging → real ending → the possibility of something more authentically one's own. The Star is available, but it follows Death. You can't rush the middle card.

options.text: They should look for work in a field that feels more meaningful and personally chosen.
options.chosen_response: That might be true but you've shifted from reading the cards to giving career advice. The Star speaks to the inner state that makes what comes next possible — renewed hope, trust in the world, a less defended openness. Stay with that.
